name: Amasa Valley
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding in central Utah's high desert where colorful agate and jasper nodules weather out of volcanic badlands. No permits required for personal collecting.
description:
Amasa Valley sits within Utah's Basin and Range province, offering accessible public land rockhounding in a remote high-desert setting. The area is scattered with volcanic deposits containing agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens that have eroded from surrounding hillsides and washes. This is classic high-desert rockhounding terrain where you can walk ridge lines and wash bottoms looking for nodules and loose pieces. The landscape is characterized by badland-style erosion exposing colorful stone layers, with good visibility for spotting specimens. Most collectors focus on dry washes and hillside surfaces where rain and wind have naturally exposed material. The remoteness means fewer crowds and plenty of untouched collecting area.

The geology here reflects Miocene-era volcanic and sedimentary activity. Silica-rich hydrothermal fluids percolated through fractures in the volcanic rocks, gradually building up layers of chalcedony and agate in cavities and along bedding planes. As the surrounding host rock eroded away over millions of years, these harder gemstone nodules were concentrated on the surface where they remain findable today. The chalky badland soils and sparse vegetation make walking and searching relatively easy compared to forested terrain.

Access is straightforward - this is unpatented BLM land with no permit requirement. You can park along access roads and hike into nearby valleys and washes. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions in this high-elevation desert zone. Summer heat can be intense, and winter snow occasionally closes higher approaches. Most collectors spend 2-4 hours per visit working a few square miles of terrain.

Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and sturdy boots since the terrain is rocky and sometimes uneven. Cell service is spotty, so let someone know where you're headed. The isolation and lack of development is part of the appeal - you're collecting in genuine remote country with outstanding views across the Great Basin.
history: Amasa Valley sits within the Basin and Range physiographic province, where Miocene volcanic activity (roughly 20-15 million years ago) deposited silica-rich fluids through fracturing host rocks. These hydrothermal systems gradually built agate and chalcedony nodules in cavities and along bedding planes. Subsequent tectonic uplift, erosion, and weathering have exposed these specimens on the surface where rockhounders find them today.
tips: Search dry washes and hillside drainages where water erosion has concentrated loose specimens; agate nodules tend to accumulate at wash margins where lighter sediment has eroded away. Look for the subtle color change where chalcedony and jasper specimens meet host rock - often a lighter or darker band indicates a specimen worth collecting. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and work methodically up a wash for 30-45 minutes before moving to the next drainage; specimen density varies but patient hunters are rewarded. Use a small hammer or crack hammer only for nodules still partly embedded - most surface finds pop free by hand or with light leverage.
terrain_notes: High-desert badlands terrain at approximately 5,200-5,600 feet elevation with sparse sagebrush, shadscale, and scattered grass. Uneven rocky footing with loose volcanic stones; walking is moderately challenging but not technical. Most parking is along dirt access roads; hiking distances typically 0.5 to 2 miles from vehicle. Ground is hard-packed caliche and clay with volcanic gravel scattered throughout. No shade except occasional gullies.
best_season: October - April. High-desert heat reaches 90-100F in summer; winter snow occasionally closes higher access roads. Spring (March-April) and fall (October-November) offer ideal 50-70F temperatures and lower sun intensity. Avoid mid-June through August unless you start before dawn.
written_directions: From Delta, Utah (Millard County seat, approximately 35 miles away), head east on US 50 toward Salina for about 20 miles. Turn south on a marked BLM access road (watch for rockhounding resource signs near mile marker 85). Follow this dirt road south for 6-8 miles into Amasa Valley proper. The road is passable in dry conditions for passenger vehicles with modest clearance but becomes rough and rutted after rain. Park at any wide spot along the access road; multiple informal pullouts exist throughout the valley. GPS coordinates (39.18, -113.37) mark the central collecting area where multiple washes and hillsides are accessible within walking distance.
road_conditions: Dirt access road, washboard in places, occasional rocks and ruts. Passable for passenger vehicles in dry weather; high-clearance or truck preferable after recent rain. Not maintained but generally open year-round.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and wide spots along the main access road throughout the valley. No developed parking lot; park on stable ground away from wash channels. Room for 2-4 vehicles at most pullouts. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or mechanized tools. No removal of fossils or cultural resources. Collect only loose material and nodules; do not hammer out specimens from intact rock faces.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No mechanized tools
No hammering into intact rock
No fossils or cultural artifacts
nearest_services: Delta, Utah, approximately 35 miles northwest. Has gas, food, water, and lodging. Salina, Utah, approximately 30 miles north. Emergency services respond from Millard County but response times are 45-60+ minutes.
accessibility_notes: Relatively uneven rocky terrain with loose volcanic gravel and scattered stones. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Hiking distances of 0.5-2 miles on natural terrain. No shade structures or facilities. Sun exposure is significant; bring hat and sunscreen. Suitable for most mobile adults and children ages 6+.

faq.question: What kind of rocks and gems can you find rockhounding near Amasa Valley, Utah?
faq.answer: Amasa Valley is known for agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. Most are found as nodules or loose fragments scattered across the badland terrain. Agate is the most sought-after find, with colors ranging from translucent whites and grays to banded oranges and browns. Jasper tends to be more opaque and comes in red, yellow, and mixed tones.

faq.question: Is there a fee or permit required for rockhounding at Amasa Valley?
faq.answer: No. Amasa Valley is located on public BLM land where recreational rockhounding is permitted at no cost. No permit is required for personal use collecting. You can park along the access road and begin hunting immediately. The only requirement is following the 25 lb per day personal use limit set by federal law.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding in Amasa Valley?
faq.answer: October through April offers the most comfortable conditions. Summer temperatures in this high-desert region regularly exceed 90F, making daytime collecting difficult. Spring (March-April) and fall (October-November) provide ideal 50-70F temperatures. Winter is possible but occasional snow may temporarily close access roads. Always check current road conditions before visiting.

faq.question: How far is Amasa Valley from the nearest town, and what do I need to bring?
faq.answer: Amasa Valley is approximately 35 miles from Delta, Utah, the nearest town with gas, food, and water. Bring at least 2-3 gallons of water per person, sun protection (hat and sunscreen), sturdy boots, a backpack or bucket for specimens, and basic hand tools like a small hammer. Cell service is spotty, so consider a satellite communicator for safety. Let someone know your destination and expected return time.

faq.question: Can beginners find agate at Amasa Valley, or do you need experience?
faq.answer: Amasa Valley is excellent for beginner rockhounders. Specimens are found on the surface and in accessible washes without specialized equipment. Simply walk hillsides and wash bottoms looking for the distinctive color and translucency of agate against lighter badland soils. Most surface finds can be picked up by hand; only use a hammer for nodules still partly embedded in soil. No advanced skills required.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do you recommend for rockhounding near Millard County's Amasa Valley?
faq.answer: Essential items include a 5-gallon bucket or backpack for specimens, 25+ lb weight capacity, sturdy hiking boots with ankle support, wide-brimmed hat, and 2-3 gallons of water. A small crack hammer and screwdriver are useful for prying out semi-embedded nodules. Bring a GPS or mark your parking area with a photo so you can navigate back across featureless terrain. Sunscreen, a light jacket for morning/evening, and a headlamp are recommended for longer visits.

meta_title: Amasa Valley Rockhounding - Find Agate in Utah
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Amasa Valley, Utah. Collect agate, jasper, and chalcedony without permits. 25 lb/day limit. Beginner-friendly high-desert collecting.
